note: Expert perspectives and further-learning resources. Viewpoints are summarized from each person's well-documented public work and attributed with a source; they are paraphrases, not direct quotes. 'level': 1=newcomer, 2=practitioner.

voices.name: Jigar Shah
voices.role: Founder, SunEdison; former Director, DOE Loan Programs Office
voices.angle: The PPA business model itself was the innovation.
voices.view: At SunEdison (2003) Shah pioneered the 'no-money-down' solar PPA: the customer buys the power, not the panels, paying a predictable price over a long contract while a financier owns the system. That shifted solar from a capital purchase to a service and unlocked a multi-billion-dollar market. Running DOE's Loan Programs Office (2021–25), he applied the same logic to first-of-a-kind clean tech — finance is the lever that turns a technology into deployment.
voices.takeaway: A PPA is a financing instrument first. The contract exists to make a project bankable.

voices.name: Tyler Norris
voices.role: Duke University, Nicholas Institute (former Cypress Creek Renewables)
voices.angle: Flexible load is cheaper headroom than new wires.
voices.view: Norris's 2025 'Rethinking Load Growth' found the existing US grid could absorb ~76 GW of new load if large users curtail just 0.25% of their annual consumption during peaks (~98 GW at 0.5%). He calls this 'curtailment-enabled headroom.' For data-center PPAs, it reframes the problem: a load willing to flex can interconnect far faster and cheaper than one that insists on firm 24/7 grid service.
voices.takeaway: Willingness to curtail is a negotiating asset — it can be the difference between a multi-year queue and a fast connection.

voices.name: Lucia Tian
voices.role: Google, Head of Advanced Energy Technologies
voices.angle: Buyers must fund the 'missing middle' of clean firm power.
voices.view: Tian leads Google's push beyond annual renewable matching toward 24/7 carbon-free energy, using offtake and investment to scale clean firm technologies — advanced nuclear (Kairos), enhanced geothermal (Fervo), carbon capture, and long-duration storage. Her thesis: large buyers should use demand to bridge the gap between a working pilot and a bankable, scalable product.
voices.takeaway: An offtake agreement can be a deliberate tool to commercialize a new technology, not just buy cheap power.

voices.name: Rich Powell
voices.angle: Corporate buyers now shape what gets built.
voices.view: Powell represents 375+ corporate buyers. Their procurement set records in 2025 (~27 GW), and the mix broadened from solar and wind into clean firm power — nuclear, geothermal, hydro, fusion, and gas with carbon capture. He argues buyers are now a primary driver of new generation and a force pushing for permitting and interconnection reform.
voices.takeaway: Aggregate corporate demand, not regulation, is increasingly steering the US generation mix.
